IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—bilingual lexicon induction under extremely low-resource supervision—and demonstrates a self-learning framework that achieves competitive results using tiny seeds (as small as 25-word seeds or numeral lists) and outperforms several prior dictionary-based mapping baselines. Its compatibility with any dictionary-based mapping method, efficient vectorized/submatrix computation for scalability, and evaluation across multiple language pairs and tasks suggest the results will influence how researchers approach low-resource cross-lingual learning and be adopted or built upon. The increased training time and memory considerations are notable but do not outweigh the method’s practical impact, so the contribution is strong though not fully transformative. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—learning bilingual lexica with minimal supervision—and demonstrates strong practical value by achieving comparable performance to richer-resource systems using as little as a 25-word seed dictionary or an automatically generated numeral list. It empirically outperforms several prior dictionary-based mapping baselines on lexicon induction and crosslingual word similarity, and the framework is general (can be combined with any dictionary-based mapper) and implemented with efficient vectorized/submatrix computations, increasing likelihood of adoption. While the iterative self-learning loop and full similarity matrix can increase time/memory costs (partially mitigated by submatrix computation), the method’s applicability to very low-resource settings and available code make it a likely influence on subsequent research and applications.

Its compatibility with any dictionary-based mapping method, efficient vectorized/submatrix computation for scalability, and evaluation across multiple language pairs and tasks suggest the results will influence how researchers approach low-resource cross-lingual learning and be adopted or built upon. The increased training time and memory considerations are notable but do not outweigh the method’s practical impact, so the contribution is strong though not fully transformative.

It empirically outperforms several prior dictionary-based mapping baselines on lexicon induction and crosslingual word similarity, and the framework is general (can be combined with any dictionary-based mapper) and implemented with efficient vectorized/submatrix computations, increasing likelihood of adoption. While the iterative self-learning loop and full similarity matrix can increase time/memory costs (partially mitigated by submatrix computation), the method’s applicability to very low-resource settings and available code make it a likely influence on subsequent research and applications.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses bilingual lexicon induction and cross-lingual embeddings, a core topic for top NLP/ML venues, and targets the appropriate audience of researchers working on cross-lingual representation learning. The formulation (iterative self-learning alternating mapping and dictionary induction) and methods (orthogonal mapping, nearest-neighbor retrieval, efficient vectorized/submatrix computations) match the methodological expectations of the field. Evaluation uses standard datasets (Dinu et al. 2015, Europarl/OPUS, RG-65, WordSim), established baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., etc.), and common metrics (lexicon induction accuracy, correlation), and code is available, indicating good reproducibility and community fit. | B(5): This paper addresses bilingual lexicon induction and cross-lingual embeddings—core topics for top NLP/ML venues—and proposes an iterative self-learning mapping that is directly relevant to that audience. It evaluates against established baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al.) on standard datasets (Dinu et al. English‑Italian, Europarl, Common Crawl) using accepted metrics (lexicon induction accuracy, crosslingual correlation), and includes practical scalability techniques (vectorized/submatrix computation) and released code. The problem formulation (orthogonal mapping + induced dictionary loop) and evaluation protocol match community standards, and the paper is positioned appropriately within the venue's research community.

English‑Italian, Europarl, Common Crawl) using accepted metrics (lexicon induction accuracy, crosslingual correlation), and includes practical scalability techniques (vectorized/submatrix computation) and released code. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes an iterative self-learning framework that alternates learning an orthogonal mapping and inducing a full bilingual dictionary, enabling operation with minimal seeds (25-word or numeral lists) and incorporating vectorized/submatrix computations for scalability. While this combination yields practical gains and outperforms several dictionary-based mapping baselines, its core components (orthogonal mapping, nearest-neighbor induction, and bootstrapped self-learning) are extensions of well-known techniques, making the contribution a moderate, incremental novelty rather than a fundamentally new idea. | B(3): The paper presents a focused, practical innovation: an iterative self-learning framework that alternates between learning an orthogonal bilingual mapping and inducing a full dictionary, along with vectorized/submatrix computation to address scalability. Its ability to work from extremely small seeds (25 words or numeral lists) and to be combined with any dictionary-based mapping are useful extensions over prior mapping baselines. However, the core components (orthogonal mapping, nearest-neighbor dictionary induction, iterative refinement) build on established techniques (e.g., Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al.) so the contribution is a non-trivial but primarily incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes an iterative self-learning framework that alternates learning an orthogonal mapping and inducing a full bilingual dictionary, enabling operation with minimal seeds (25-word or numeral lists) and incorporating vectorized/submatrix computations for scalability. While this combination yields practical gains and outperforms several dictionary-based mapping baselines, its core components (orthogonal mapping, nearest-neighbor induction, and bootstrapped self-learning) are extensions of well-known techniques, making the contribution a moderate, incremental novelty rather than a fundamentally new idea.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a focused, practical innovation: an iterative self-learning framework that alternates between learning an orthogonal bilingual mapping and inducing a full dictionary, along with vectorized/submatrix computation to address scalability. Its ability to work from extremely small seeds (25 words or numeral lists) and to be combined with any dictionary-based mapping are useful extensions over prior mapping baselines. However, the core components (orthogonal mapping, nearest-neighbor dictionary induction, iterative refinement) build on established techniques (e.g., Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al.) so the contribution is a non-trivial but primarily incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: the method is tested across multiple corpora (Dinu et al., Europarl/OPUS, SdeWaC, Common Crawl) and on both bilingual lexicon induction and crosslingual word-similarity (RG-65, WordSim-353), and compared to a wide set of strong baselines. The paper discusses limitations (increased training time from the self-learning loop and memory issues for the full similarity matrix, with a submatrix solution) and releases code. However, important analysis is missing: hyperparameters are not reported and no explicit ablation/sensitivity studies are described, which weakens reproducibility and understanding of which components drive gains. | B(4): The paper evaluates the method across multiple languages and datasets (Dinu et al. English–Italian, Europarl-derived dictionaries for EN–DE/EN–FI, Common Crawl/SdeWaC corpora) and compares against several strong baselines, demonstrating robustness especially with very small seed dictionaries. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and code is available, which aids reproducibility; however, hyperparameters are not reported and there is no mention of detailed ablation or sensitivity analyses in the summary. The authors discuss practical limitations (increased training time due to the iterative loop and memory issues requiring submatrix computation), so while the empirical evaluation is strong, missing hyperparameter details and limited ablation leave some reproducibility and analysis gaps.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by experiments on multiple datasets (Dinu et al., Europarl-derived, Common Crawl) and comparisons to strong baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., etc.), and the proposed iterative Procrustes-style orthogonal mapping and nearest-neighbor induction is a standard, technically sound approach. The paper addresses scalability with vectorized/submatrix computation and provides code, indicating reproducibility. However, some experimental details are missing in the summary (hyperparameter reporting absent and no mention of statistical significance testing), which are minor methodological gaps that do not fundamentally undermine the reported results. Overall the technical treatment is sound with only minor issues to clarify. | B(4): The core claims are supported by empirical evidence: the iterative self-learning procedure is evaluated against multiple baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., etc.) on several datasets (English–Italian, English–German, Finnish, RG-65, WordSim-353) and is reported to outperform them, including with very small seed dictionaries; code and limitations (runtime, memory) are disclosed. However, the summary does not report mathematical derivations or proofs, and important experimental details (hyperparameter settings, statistical significance tests) are missing, which reduces reproducibility and makes some methodological choices harder to evaluate. Given these minor but important gaps, the technical claims appear valid overall but not perfectly documented.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and presents its main contributions early (the iterative self-learning framework and its claims are stated up front). Notation and methodology are clearly described (orthogonal mapping W, preprocessing, nearest-neighbor induction, and submatrix computation) and the experiments are thorough across datasets and baselines. Minor presentation issues remain — hyperparameters are not reported and writing is described as only "adequate" — but related work and limitations are clearly discussed. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodological flow (preprocessing, orthogonal mapping W, iterative dictionary induction) and thorough experiments (datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics) and conclusions, so structure is logical. The main contributions (self-learning framework, handling very small seeds, vectorized/submatrix computations) are stated early and limitations are explicitly discussed. Notation seems consistent and defined around the mapping W and preprocessing steps. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables/examples are provided or fully effective; overall writing quality is described as adequate.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate and recent baselines (Mikolov et al. 2013; Xing et al. 2015; Zhang et al. 2016; Artetxe et al. 2016; Lazaridou et al. 2015; Luong et al. 2015; BilBOWA) and evaluates on standard datasets (Dinu et al. 2015 English–Italian, Europarl/OPUS-derived dictionaries, SdeWaC/Common Crawl) using the same metrics (accuracy, correlation). Comparisons appear fair (same data and evaluation metrics) and the paper explicitly reports outperforming several dictionary-based baselines while noting comparable results to systems using richer resources; related work coverage is described as thorough. Overall the comparisons are comprehensive and well contextualized. | B(4): The paper compares to a broad and relevant set of baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Zhang et al., Artetxe et al., Lazaridou et al., Luong et al., BilBOWA) and evaluates on multiple standard datasets (Dinu et al. English–Italian, Europarl-derived pairs, SdeWaC/Common Crawl subsets, RG-65, WordSim-353) using common metrics (accuracy, correlation). Comparisons appear fair—same data and metrics are used, code is available, and related work coverage is described as thorough—and the authors report outperforming several dictionary-based mapping baselines, particularly with small seed dictionaries. A minor omission is that hyperparameters are not reported (which can affect reproducibility/fairness), and the summary does not explicitly state where prior methods perform comparably or better.

main_claims: Introduce a self-learning framework that iteratively alternates between learning a bilingual embedding mapping and inducing a full bilingual dictionary to improve bilingual lexicon induction.

The proposed method works with very small amounts of bilingual evidence (as little as a 25-word seed dictionary or an automatically generated numeral list) and obtains results comparable to systems using much richer resources.
The framework can be combined with any dictionary-based mapping technique and uses efficient, vectorized computations (including submatrix computation) to address scalability and memory issues.
The method outperforms several prior dictionary-based mapping baselines on bilingual lexicon induction and crosslingual word similarity, especially when using small seed dictionaries.
_summary.methodology: Iteratively learn an orthogonal linear mapping W between preprocessed (length-normalized, mean-centered) monolingual embedding matrices using a seed bilingual dictionary, induce a full dictionary by nearest-neighbor retrieval (dot-product similarity) from the mapped source into the target, replace the seed with the induced dictionary, and repeat until convergence; employ vectorized matrix multiplication and submatrix computation for efficiency.
datasets: Dinu et al. (2015) public English-Italian dataset (monolingual embeddings + bilingual dictionary)
Europarl-derived dictionaries from OPUS (used to create training/test sets for English-Italian, English-German, Finnish pairs)
SdeWaC corpus (9 billion words, WaCky collection)
Common Crawl corpus (WMT2016 Common Crawl, 8/2.8 billion words depending on language)
limitations: The iterative self-learning loop increases total training time because learning is enclosed in a loop and requires building the full induced dictionary at each iteration.
The full similarity matrix XWZ^T can be too large to fit in memory for large vocabularies, necessitating submatrix computation to avoid memory issues.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - bilingual word embeddings / bilingual lexicon induction
